name: Flight 19 Disappearance
description: Flight 19 refers to the disappearance of a group of five U.S. Navy TBM Avenger torpedo bombers during a training flight over the Atlantic Ocean on December 5, 1945. The flight, led by experienced instructor Lieutenant Charles Carroll Taylor, departed from Naval Air Station Fort Lauderdale, Florida. All 14 airmen on board were lost, as was a PBM Mariner flying boat with a 13-man crew sent out to search for them. The official Navy report attributed the loss to pilot error, specifically Taylor's disorientation and misjudgment of position leading to the aircraft running out of fuel. However, the lack of wreckage and the unusual circumstances surrounding the incident have fueled numerous alternative theories. Proponents suggest everything from magnetic anomalies and time warps to alien abduction and involvement of the Bermuda Triangle. These theories often cite alleged radio transmissions indicating confusion and equipment malfunctions, as well as the inexperience of some crew members. Mainstream analysis, however, emphasizes Taylor's documented history of getting lost, the challenging weather conditions, and the inherent dangers of overwater navigation at the time. The official report, while criticized for some inconsistencies, remains the most widely accepted explanation. The incident has become a staple of paranormal and conspiracy narratives, contributing to the mystique of the Bermuda Triangle.
subtitle: Vanishing squadron over the Atlantic.

claims.claim: The Bermuda Triangle caused the disappearance.
claims.evidence: The location of the disappearance within the Bermuda Triangle and the area's reputation for unusual events.
claims.criticism: The Bermuda Triangle's anomalous properties are largely anecdotal and statistically insignificant; disappearances occur worldwide.

claims.claim: A magnetic anomaly or time warp swallowed the planes.
claims.evidence: Alleged radio transmissions suggesting unusual instrument readings and disorientation.
claims.criticism: These radio transmissions are subject to interpretation and could be attributed to equipment malfunction or pilot error under stress. No physical evidence supports a time warp.

claims.evidence: The complete lack of wreckage and the mysterious nature of the event.
claims.criticism: The lack of wreckage is not unusual for aircraft lost at sea, especially given the conditions and limited search capabilities at the time. There is no credible evidence of alien involvement.

keyFigures.name: Lawrence David Kusche
keyFigures.role: Author of 'The Bermuda Triangle Mystery - Solved', a skeptical investigation

keyFigures.name: Gian J. Quasar
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Into the Bermuda Triangle: Pursuing the Truth Behind the World's Most Enduring Mystery'

timeline.event: PBM Mariner search plane also disappears
Flight 19 becomes a prominent case in Bermuda Triangle lore
